commits.physical_specs_summary: Low frame, lever-driven pedals, smaller wheels than high-wheeler, solid rubber tires, ~65.5 lbs
commits.functional_specs_summary: Rider near ground; lever propulsion; marketed as 'safety'; experimental

objective_pressures.label: improved stability
objective_pressures.description: Lower seat/wheels reduce center of gravity and fall risk
objective_pressures.notes: Patent and summaries state safety as goal

objective_pressures.label: mechanical simplicity (lever drive)
objective_pressures.description: Levers avoid chain complexities of the era

commits.notes: Small-scale production; not a market success. Chain drive not yet present.

commits.physical_specs_summary: Chain-driven rear wheel; centrally mounted pedals; smaller front wheel; solid rubber tires; 'bridle-rod' steering
commits.functional_specs_summary: Rear-wheel chain drive improves efficiency; retains dwarf proportions; marketed as distinct from high-wheeler

objective_pressures.label: increased drivetrain efficiency
objective_pressures.description: Chain drive transfers power more effectively than levers

objective_pressures.label: manufacturing refinement
objective_pressures.description: Chain drive simpler to produce at scale than lever linkage
objective_pressures.notes: Inferred from trade note on limited commercial uptake

commits.diff_summary_from_parent: Change: lever drive replaced by chain drive to rear wheel. Likely drivers: improved mechanical efficiency, simpler power transmission. Evidence: UK Patent 3924 (1879), Science Museum object. Uncertainty: why rear wheel larger than front; why equal wheel size not yet adopted.

causal_claims.statement: Bicyclette hailed by Coventry trade as first rear-drive safety

commits.notes: Commercial production limited (Rudge); established rear-chain topology as promising.

commits.physical_specs_summary: Diamond frame; equal-sized wheels (26–30") rear chain drive; solid rubber tires; saddle over rear axle
commits.functional_specs_summary: Stable geometry; easy mounting; standardized gearing; mass-produced; marketed as 'safety bicycle'

objective_pressures.label: improved stability and handling
objective_pressures.description: Equal wheel sizes and low center of gravity improve balance, reduce tipping

objective_pressures.label: manufacturing standardization
objective_pressures.description: Single wheel size, simple frame reduce parts complexity
objective_pressures.notes: Direct from design; scale inferred from adoption

objective_pressures.label: cost reduction
objective_pressures.description: Simpler frame and fewer variants lower production cost vs high-wheelers/tricycles
objective_pressures.notes: Price context from secondary trade histories

subjective_pressures.label: perceived safety
subjective_pressures.description: Public perceived 'safety' bicycles as less dangerous; social acceptability grew
subjective_pressures.notes: Advertising uses 'safety' claim; commentary notes reduced danger

subjective_pressures.label: fashion/modernity signal
subjective_pressures.description: New machine symbolized modern, progressive mobility

commits.diff_summary_from_parent: Change: equal-sized wheels and diamond frame adopted; chain drive standardized. Likely drivers: stability, ease of use, cost reduction; 'safety' marketing strong. Evidence: Rover museum object, Graces Guide, 1885 advertisement. Uncertainty: whether marketing preceded or followed rider confidence.

causal_claims.statement: Rover design 'set the pattern to the world' (Cycling Magazine)

commits.notes: Archetypal 'safety bicycle' that dominated late 1880s markets.

commits.physical_specs_summary: Diamond frame, chain drive, equal wheels; pneumatic inflatable rubber tires (Dunlop, 1888); some rim brakes
commits.functional_specs_summary: Smoother ride, faster on rough roads, less vibration; longer rides feasible; expanded user base (including women); added air-pressure maintenance

objective_pressures.label: comfort improvement
objective_pressures.description: Pneumatic tires absorb road shocks, reducing rider fatigue

objective_pressures.label: speed increase
objective_pressures.description: Lower rolling resistance vs solid rubber

subjective_pressures.label: reduced safety anxieties
subjective_pressures.description: Public perception of cycling safety improved due to comfort/control; 'bicycle face' debate shows health concerns were salient
subjective_pressures.notes: Newspaper commentary reflects concerns; pneumatic tires positioned as healthier

subjective_pressures.label: social acceptability for women
subjective_pressures.description: Comfort helped make cycling acceptable for women; marketed accordingly
subjective_pressures.notes: Scholarship shows deliberate women's marketing; contemporaneous ads would strengthen

subjective_pressures.label: aspirational modernity
subjective_pressures.description: Pneumatic-tired safeties symbolized cutting-edge technology and personal advancement
subjective_pressures.notes: From period magazine tone; needs more primary ad evidence

commits.diff_summary_from_parent: Change: pneumatic tires adopted over solid rubber. Likely drivers: comfort and speed gains; secondary effect: expanded user base (women, older riders). Evidence: Dunlop history, cycling magazines. Uncertainty: exact diffusion speed; whether comfort alone explains boom vs. marketing amplification.

causal_claims.statement: Dunlop's pneumatic tire was a key enabler of the 1890s cycling boom
Bicycle became socially acceptable for women partly due to comfort improvements

commits.notes: Shows subsystem (tire) adoption into main artifact lineage.

commits.physical_specs: Solid rubber tire, glued/riveted to wood/iron wheel; no cushioning

pressures.label: simple construction
pressures.notes: Museum object descriptions show solid construction

commits.physical_specs: Inflatable rubber tube inside outer tire; vulcanized rubber; requires pump/patching

pressures.label: comfort and speed
pressures.notes: Race demonstrations and period accounts confirm

pressures.label: reduced safety anxieties
pressures.notes: Contemporary health debates show concerns existed; tires marketed as healthier
